questions.question: What is the main purpose of the sifting phase in BB84?

options.text: To keep only bits where Alice and Bob used the same basis

questions.explanation: Sifting identifies measurements where Alice and Bob happened to choose the same basis. Only these positions have correlated results (same bit values in absence of noise). Mismatched-basis measurements give random results and must be discarded.

questions.question: Why is it safe to publicly announce the basis choices during sifting?

options.text: Knowing the basis doesn't reveal which specific state was used
Eve cannot hear the classical channel
The announcement happens after the key is used

questions.explanation: The basis only indicates whether Z or X was used, but each basis contains two states (e.g., |0⟩ and |1⟩ in Z-basis). Knowing Alice used Z-basis doesn't reveal whether she sent |0⟩ or |1⟩. The bit value remains secret.

questions.question: What fraction of bits typically survives sifting in BB84?
questions.explanation: With two equally-probable bases, Alice and Bob independently each have 50% chance of choosing Z or X. They match 50% of the time: P(match) = P(both Z) + P(both X) = 0.25 + 0.25 = 0.5.

questions.question: What happens to bits used for QBER estimation?

options.text: They become part of the final key
They are discarded because their values were revealed
They are sent to Bob again

questions.explanation: During QBER estimation, Alice publicly reveals her bit values for the sample. Since Eve can now learn these values, they cannot be used for the key and must be discarded. Only the remaining unrevealed bits proceed to error correction.

questions.question: What is the typical QBER threshold for BB84?
questions.explanation: The security threshold for BB84 is approximately 11% (varies slightly by attack model). Above this QBER, Eve can potentially have more information than can be removed by privacy amplification, making secure key generation impossible.

questions.question: Why is a larger QBER sample more accurate but potentially wasteful?

options.text: It takes more time to process
More bits are sacrificed (revealed) and cannot be used for the key
Eve can attack a larger sample more easily

questions.explanation: Larger samples give more accurate QBER estimates (smaller confidence intervals), but every bit in the sample is revealed publicly and must be discarded. There's a tradeoff between estimation accuracy and remaining key length.

questions.question: After sifting, Alice's and Bob's keys should be identical if there was no noise or eavesdropping.
questions.explanation: True. When Alice and Bob use the same basis, Bob's measurement deterministically yields Alice's bit value (for ideal conditions). Any differences indicate noise (channel errors) or eavesdropping—this is exactly what QBER measures.
